coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of FeCO3/nitrogen-doped carbon dot composites for lithium-ion battery anodes
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Bin
Liang, Yeru
Yu, Xiaoyuan
Yuan, Gang
Zheng, Mingtao
Xiao, Yong
Dong, Hanwu
Liu, Yingliang
Hu, Hang
coredata.dc:description: FeCO3 has attracted increasing attention as an anode material for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) due to its high capacity, abundance and low cost. However, the poor electronic conductivity and slow ion diffusion dynamics of FeCO3 limit its rate performance. To address this issue, a FeCO3/nitrogen-doped carbon dot (FeCO3/N-CD) composite applied in LIBs is developed by a facile one-step hydrothermal method. It is found that the introduction of N-CD effectively inhibits the crystal growth and stacking of FeCO3, leading to the formation of a unique nanorod composite with hierarchical porous structure. Benefiting from the rational structure, the as-prepared FeCO3/N-CD composite shows significantly improved electrochemical performances when used as anode materials of LIBs. For example, a stable reversible capacity of 872 mA h g−1 after 460 cycles at a current density of 200 mA g−1 can be achieved, which is much higher than that of the pristine FeCO3. This study provides an efficient route to prepare advanced FeCO3-based composites for anode materials in high-performance LIBs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Anode materials
Ferrous carbonate
Carbon quantum dots
Cycling stability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of FeCO3; (b) and (d) HR-TEM images of FeCO3; (c) and (e) HR-TEM images of FeCO3/N-CD-15; (f) TEM image of N-CDs. The inset in f shows the excitation spectrum (monitored at 464 nm) and emission spectrum (excited at 382 nm) of the N-CDs. Photographs of N-CDs in daylight (left) and UV light at 365 nm (right).

(a) XRD patterns of N-CDs, FeCO3, FeCO3/N-CD-10, FeCO3/N-CD-15, and FeCO3/N-CD-20; (b) FTIR spectra of N-CDs, FeCO3 and FeCO3/N-CD-15; (c) and (d) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of FeCO3/N-CD-15 and FeCO3. Inset: Barrett-Joyner-Halenda pore size distribution of FeCO3 and FeCO3/N-CD-15.
(a) Survey spectrum; (b) Fe 2p spectrum; (c) C 1s spectrum; (d) O 1s spectrum; (e) N 1s spectrum; (f) TGA analysis of FeCO3 and FeCO3/N-CD-15 composite.

(a) Discharge-charge plots of the initial cycle at 200 mA g−1 of electrodes using FeCO3/N-CD composites (FeCO3, FeCO3/N-CD-10, FeCO3/N-CD-15 and FeCO3/N-CD-20); (b) cycling performances of FeCO3/N-CD composites.
Rate capacities of the pristine FeCO3 and FeCO3/N-CD-15 anodes at different current densities.

(a) Cyclic voltammetry curves of FeCO3/N-CD-15; (b) the discharge-charge curves of the 20th, 60th, 80th, 120th, 180th and 300th cycles; (c) and (d) the differential capacity vs. voltage plots of the 20th, 60th, 80th, 120th, 180th and 300th cycles in region Ⅱ (charge) and region I (discharge); (e) cycling performances of FeCO3/N-CD-15; (f) the process of Li+ transport in FeCO3/N-CD composites.

Schematic illustration of the formation process for FeCO3/N-CD composites.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of FeCO3/nitrogen-doped carbon dot composites for lithium-ion battery anodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou, 510642, Guangdong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou, 510642, Guangdong, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: FeCO3 has attracted increasing attention as an anode material for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) due to its high capacity, abundance and low cost. However, the poor electronic conductivity and slow ion diffusion dynamics of FeCO3 limit its rate performance. To address this issue, a FeCO3/nitrogen-doped carbon dot (FeCO3/N-CD) composite applied in LIBs is developed by a facile one-step hydrothermal method. It is found that the introduction of N-CD effectively inhibits the crystal growth and stacking of FeCO3, leading to the formation of a unique nanorod composite with hierarchical porous structure. Benefiting from the rational structure, the as-prepared FeCO3/N-CD composite shows significantly improved electrochemical performances when used as anode materials of LIBs. For example, a stable reversible capacity of 872 mA h g−1 after 460 cycles at a current density of 200 mA g−1 can be achieved, which is much higher than that of the pristine FeCO3. This study provides an efficient route to prepare advanced FeCO3-based composites for anode materials in high-performance LIBs.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the global energy shortage and warming climate have become increasingly prominent problems. Safe, environmentally friendly and high-performance energy storage systems can significantly ameliorate the world’s energy and environmental problems [1,2]. Rechargeable lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are one of the most used new energy storage systems, because of their outstanding features of small size, high energy density, remarkable cycle stability, lack of a memory effect, and low self-discharge rate [3–5]. However, the theoretical capacity of the commercial anode material graphite is only 372 mA h g−1, and it has a low rate capability; thus, graphite cannot meet the increasing demands for high-energy-density LIBs [6,7]. Therefore, development of new anode materials with high reversible capacities has stimulated worldwide research activities.

Recently, transition metal carbonates (TMCs) including MnCO3, CoCO3, ZnCO3 and FeCO3 were proposed as promising anode materials due to their high theoretical capacity, low cost and ease of preparation [8–13]. The high capacity of TMCs is related to the following reaction mechanism:
MCO 3
+ 2 L
i +
↔ M +
Li 2
CO 3
;
Li 2
CO 3
+
4 + 0.5 x
L
i +
↔ 3
Li 2
O + 0.5
Li x
C
2
,
x = 0,1,2 . It is worthwhile to note that the C4+ in Li2CO3 can be converted into C0 or other low-valence states of C (LixC2) with the catalyst of the just-formed metal nanoparticles in the first reaction step [14,15]. Because Li2CO3 can be considered an active electrode material during the electrochemical process, TMCs usually show extremely high reversible capacities. Among these TMCs, ferrous carbonate (FeCO3) is recognized as a high-efficiency lithium-ion battery anode material because of its better activity when compared with other TMCs [16–23]. Nevertheless, like other TMCs, the current FeCO3 anode usually suffers from poor conductivity and large volume changes in FeCO3 electrodes during the lithiation/delithiation process. These drawbacks result in poor rate performance and inferior long-term cyclizing behavior, which limit its wide-spread application in LIBs.

To address the above-mentioned issues, many strategies for FeCO3 anode materials have been exploited. One of the widely used methods is construction of hierarchical nanostructured FeCO3-based anode materials to enhance the rate performance and cycling stability of FeCO3 [13,19,20]. For example, Zhou et al. fabricated rambutan-like FeCO3 hollow microspheres that displayed a reversible capacity of 710 mA h g−1 after 200 cycles at a current density of 200 mA g−1 [13]. The other effective approaches are to combine active materials with conductive materials such as carbon fiber [21], graphene [16,22,23] and CNTs [24]. Yu et al. reported FeCO3/graphene composites with a reversible capacity of 789 m A h g−1 after 80 cycles without capacity loss [22]. Despite these remarkable progresses, the preparation processes of these FeCO3-based anodes are often featured with complicated synthetic steps. Moreover, the reproducibility and the uniformity for the FeCO3-based composite structure are also unimpressive. Therefore, it is of great interest to develop an efficient strategy to prepare advanced FeCO3-based anodes for LIBs.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a class of zero-dimensional carbon materials, have become increasingly popular in the fields of materials [25,26], agriculture [27,28], biotechnology [29,30] and nanomedicine [31,32] due to their unique structural features and properties. In recent years, CDs were successfully employed to prepare nanocomposites with improved electrochemical performances. The applications of CDs in supercapacitors, LIBs and sodium-ion batteries have been studied and aroused increasing research interest [33–36].

In this work, a new type of FeCO3-based anode, i.e., FeCO3/nitrogen-doped carbon dot (FeCO3/N-CD) composite is developed by a facile one-step hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). It is found that the introduction of N-CDs effectively inhibits the crystal growth and the stacking of FeCO3, leading to the formation of a unique nanorod composite with hierarchical porous structure. Such a composite structure can provide a larger active surface area for electrochemical reactions and greatly shorten the diffusion paths of Li+ ions, which contributes to a higher specific capacity and rate capability. Furthermore, the existence of N-CDs can relieve the volume expansions in the lithiation/delithiation process, yielding an improved cyclic stability. Last but not the least, the incorporation of N-CDs into FeCO3 nanorod is found to be effective in improving the conductivity of the resultant composites. Benefiting from the rational structure, the as-prepared FeCO3/N-CD composite shows significantly enhanced electrochemical performances when used as anode materials of LIBs. For example, a stable reversible capacity of 872 mA h g−1 after 460 cycles at a current density of 200 mA g−1 can be achieved, which is higher than of the pristine FeCO3.
Citric acid (99.5%), urea (99%) and FeSO4·7H2O (99%) were purchased from Aladdin. All reagents were used without further purification.

Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared using a one-step hydrothermal approach according to a reported method [37]. Briefly, 3.0 g of citric acid and 1.0 g of urea were dissolved in deionized water. After ultrasonic treatment for 2 h, the mixture was transferred into a 50-mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 5 h. The solution was cooled to room temperature, and the black solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to remove large particles. The supernatant was collected and freeze-dried. Finally, a black power product (i.e., N-CDs) was obtained.

FeCO3/N-CD composites were prepared using a one-step hydrothermal approach. Typically, N-CDs was dispersed in 40 mL of deionized water via an ultrasonic process. 10 mL of FeSO4·7H2O (0.78 M) and 2.4 g of urea were slowly added to the mixture under moderate stirring for 30 min. Then, the obtained mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and held at 100 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the precipitates were collected by filtration and washed with distilled water and ethanol five times. After dried at 60 °C for 18 h, the targeted FeCO3/N-CD-x composite was obtained, in which x represented the weight percentage of N-CDs in the FeCO3/N-CD composite. Specifically, the addition of 100, 160, 226 mg of N-CDs yielded the FeCO3/N-CD-10, FeCO3/N-CD-15 and FeCO3/N-CD-20 in this work, respectively.

The morphologies and microstructure of FeCO3/N-CD-15 composites and pristine FeCO3 were studied by scanning electron microscopy (ZEISS Sigma 500), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2100F, 200 kV) technologies. X-ray diffraction characterization (XD-2, Beijing Purkinje General) operated at 36 kV was employed to evaluate the crystal structure of FeCO3/N-CD composites. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250 electron spectrometer, 150-W Al Kα radiation) was carried out to investigate the elemental compositions and valence states. The thermal decomposition behavior of the samples was characterized by a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, PerkinElmer, Pyris Diamond TG/DTA) at a heating rate of 10 °C/min in air.

CR2025 half coin cells were assembled using FeCO3/N-CD composites as anode materials. The Electrodes were fabricated as follows. In a moderate amount of NMP, FeCO3/N-CDs, acetylene black and poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF) were mixed at a weight ratio of 70:20:10. The mixture was ground in a ball mill, and a black slurry was obtained. The slurry was cast onto a copper foil and vacuum dried at 80 °C for 12 h. The electrode generally has an active material loading of 2–3 mg cm−2. A 1-M LiPF6 solution in a mixed solvent of ethylene carbonate (EC) and dimethyl carbonate (DMC) (1:1, v/v) was used as the electrolyte. All the half coin cells were assembled in an argon-filled glove box. A BTS53 battery testing system (Netware Technology Limited) was used to carry out the charge-discharge experiments in the potential range from 0.05 to 3.0 V and a current density of 200 mA g−1. The cyclic voltammograms (CVs) were collected on a potentiostat/galvanostat model (PerkinElmer 273A, EG&E) in the potential range of 0–3 V with a scan rate of 0.2 mV s−1. An IM6e electrochemical workstation was used to conduct electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with an oscillating voltage of 5 mV and a frequency range from 10 Hz to 100 kHz.

As a proof-of-concept demonstration, the FeCO3/N-CD-15 was first studied in detail. In order to evaluate the role of the N-CD in the resulting composite, a control sample, i.e., a pristine FeCO3 was synthesized without the presence of N-CDs. The morphologies of the pristine FeCO3 and FeCO3/N-CD-15 were investigated by SEM, TEM and HR-TEM technologies. As shown in Fig. 1 a and Fig. 1b, the pristine FeCO3 shows regular nanorod morphology with a length ranging from 200 to 800 nm. The pristine FeCO3 nanorods have smooth edges and are prone to stack together compactly. The observed lattice fringes of 0.2795 nm from the HR-TEM image are indexed to the interplanar spacing of the (104) planes for the pristine FeCO3 (Fig. 1d).

While with the addition of the N-CDs, the as-prepared FeCO3/N-CD-15 possesses a much smaller size of FeCO3 nanorods with a uniform dispersion of N-CDs, as shown in Fig. 1c and S1a. The loose aggregates of FeCO3/N-CD-15 particles are composed of short nanorods with a length ranging from 40 to 60 nm, accompanying with a number of N-CDs attached to the FeCO3 surfaces. This result suggests that the introduction of N-CDs can suppress the growth of FeCO3 and protect the nanorods from tight stacking. During the hydrothermal process, the Fe2+ ions react with CO3 2− from the decomposition of urea, producing the FeCO3. In the growth process of the FeCO3 crystal, the N-CDs are attached to the surface of the FeCO3 nanorods (Scheme 1). The adsorption of N-CDs on the surface inhibits the crystal growth and protects the nanorods from tight stacking, finally forming the hierarchical porous nanocomposites. Such a unique hierarchical porous structure of the FeCO3/N-CD-15 may facilitate the transfer of Li+ and electrons and impair volume expansions of the anode material during the lithiation/delithiation process. The HR-TEM in Fig. 1e shows a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm in the surfaces and interspaces of FeCO3, corresponding to the N-CD (100) planes. The size of the N-CDs is approximately 3 nm, which coincides with that of the original N-CDs (Fig. 1f). The inset in Fig. 1f shows that the N-CDs have optimal emission and excitation wavelengths at 464 and 382 nm, respectively, and yield a blue color under UV light at 365 nm. The structure of FeCO3/N-CD-15 was further examined by EDX mapping images, as shown in Fig. S1b-f. The element distributions of C, O, Fe and N reveal a uniform dispersion of N-CDs in the resulting FeCO3/N-CD-15 composite.

Fig. 2 a depicts the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the as-obtained FeCO3, N-CDs, and FeCO3/N-CD composites. For the N-CDs, a broad peak at approximately 27.5° is observed, which corresponds to highly defective or disordered carbons [36]. For the pristine FeCO3, the major diffraction peaks coincide with the standard data from JCPDS no. 29–0696. There is a prominent diffraction peak centered at 32.0°, corresponding to the (104) plane of siderite FeCO3 [17,24]. For the FeCO3/N-CDs, the peak centered at 22° corresponds to the (203) plane of Fe2O3 (JCPDS no. 25–1402). Moreover, this diffraction intensity increases as the number of N-CDs increases. In our experiment, the Fe2O3 is generated by the oxidation of the N-CDs. That is to say, the oxygen-containing functional groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl, epoxy groups, etc.) on the surface of the N-CDs can react with Fe2+ to produce Fe2O3. As a result, the degree of crystallization of the obtained Fe2O3 is improved when increasing the quantity of the N-CDs filled in. In addition, the other peaks are well indexed to siderite FeCO3. It can also be observed that as the number of N-CDs increases, there is no obvious change in the diffraction intensity of the (104) plane of siderite FeCO3, suggesting that the crystalization of siderite FeCO3 remains unchanged.

The FTIR spectra of the FeCO3, N-CDs and FeCO3/N-CD-15 are shown in Fig. 2b. For the N-CDs, the peaks at 3465 cm−1, 3138 cm−1, 1704 cm−1, 1620 cm−1 and 1407 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of –OH, C–H, CO, CN and N–H, respectively, due to the presence of nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of the N-CDs (i.e., pyrrolic N and pyridinic N) [38]. Compared with the pristine FeCO3, the absorption bands for FeCO3/N-CD-15 at approximately 1600 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 are broader, which results from the infrared absorption by the functional groups in the N-CDs. The formation of FeCO3/N-CD composites can be confirmed by the stretching vibration of CN at 1704 cm−1. Fig. 2c shows the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of FeCO3/N-CDs. The surface area of the FeCO3/N-CDs is 91.22 m2 g−1, which is higher than that of the pristine FeCO3 (65.08 m2 g−1, Fig. 2d). As shown in the inset of Fig. 2c, it can be observed that FeCO3/N-CD-15 mainly consists of micropores centered at 1.5 nm and mesopores in the range of 7–11 nm. These results are consistent with the TEM and HR-TEM results. Based on these results, it can be demonstrated that the addition of N-CDs can facilitate an increase in surface area and the modification of the pore structure of FeCO3/N-CD composites, which can afford more active sites for improving the electrochemical performance.

To further confirm the surface electronic state of the FeCO3/N-CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used. The XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 3 a demonstrates the existence of Fe, O, N and C. The content of N is 7.78%. Fig. 3b–e shows the spectra of Fe 2p, C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s. In Fig. 3b, two peaks centered at 725.0 eV and 710.8 eV corresponding to the binding energies of Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2, respectively, are attributed to the presence of Fe2+ [17]. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum of FeCO3/N-CD-15 show three peaks at 288.5 eV, 286.6 eV and 284.8 eV, which can be ascribed to CO/CN, C–O/C–N and CC/C–C, respectively (Fig. 3c) [36,38,39]. As shown the high-resolution O 1s spectrum in Fig. 3d, the peaks at 532.3 eV, 531.6 eV and 530.1 eV can be assigned to C–OH, C–O–C and CO, respectively, suggesting the existence of abundant oxygen-containing groups [6]. In Fig. 3e, two peaks at 400.4 eV and 399.6 eV can be assigned to the pyrrolic N and pyridinic N, respectively [39]. TGA was used to determine the content of the N-CDs in the FeCO3/N-CD composites. As presented in Fig. 2f, the weight loss of the pristine FeCO3 is 26%, which is considered the decomposition of FeCO3 to Fe2O3 [40]. Considering the weight loss of the N-CDs is 100% in the same experimental condition, the additional weight loss of FeCO3/N-CD-15 is believed to result from the decomposition of the N-CDs, and thus the content of N-CDs in the FeCO3/N-CD-15 composite can be calculated to be ∼19%.

To investigate the lithium ion storage behaviors, half coin cells based on FeCO3 and FeCO3/N-CD anodes were assembled and tested. The initial discharge-charge curves at a current density of 200 mA g−1 for electrodes using FeCO3, FeCO3/N-CD-10, FeCO3/N-CD-15 and FeCO3/N-CD-20 anodes are depicted in Fig. 4 a. In the first discharge process, there is a main flat voltage area at 0.8 V, which is assigned to the reduction of Fe2+ in FeCO3 to Fe0 [24]. For the pristine FeCO3 anode, a specific charge capacity is only 733 mA h g−1, corresponding to a Coulomb efficiency of 59.4%. For the FeCO3/N-CD-10, FeCO3/N-CD-15 and FeCO3/N-CD-20 anodes, the specific capacities are significantly enhanced to 1112, 1815, and 1404 mA h g−1, giving Coulomb efficiencies of 45.1%. 46.4%, and 46.1%, respectively. The low initial Coulomb efficiency is mainly caused by the formation of SEI films [41–43]. Notably, the initial Coulomb efficiency of FeCO3/N-CDs is lower than that of FeCO3, possibly due to the reduction reactions of N-CDs (i.e., the partial decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups), which leads to the irreversible capacities of the anodes [44]. The coulombic efficiencies of the FeCO3/N-CD-10, FeCO3/N-CD-15 and FeCO3/N-CD-20 electrodes change in a narrow range (45.1%–46.4%), and these small changes may be caused by technological differences in battery assembly. Because the N-CD content is low and the reduction reactions of the N-CDs are only a small part of the irreversible reactions, the difference in the N-CD content may not be enough to obviously change the initial Coulomb efficiency. Fig. 4b shows the cycling performances of the pristine FeCO3 and FeCO3/N-CD anodes at 200 mA g−1 between 0.05 and 3 V. It is found that the capacities of the anode materials decrease sharply due to SEI film formation and electrolyte degradation in the first 20 cycles [24,45]. After 120 cycles, the stable reversible capacities of FeCO3, FeCO3/N-CD-10, FeCO3/N-CD-15, and FeCO3/N-CD-20 are 343, 253, 612, and 447 mA h g−1, respectively. Obviously, the content of N-CDs plays an important role in the capacities. When the N-CD weight percentage is 10%, the discharge capacity of FeCO3/N-CD-10 is lower than that of the pristine FeCO3. This is because the composites contain such small amounts of N-CDs that it is not enough for a remarkable synergic effect on the components. As the content of the N-CDs increases, the specific capacity of the FeCO3/N-CD-15 anode significantly increases to 612 mA h g−1. When the weight percentage of the N-CDs reaches 20%, and the specific capacity of the composite anode decreases to 447 mA h g−1. These results suggest that an excessive or insufficient N-CD content does not promote good performance. There is an optimal content of N-CDs in the FeCO3/N-CD composites, and FeCO3/N-CD-15 exhibits the best electrochemical performance. Interestingly, the specific capacity of FeCO3/N-CD-15 descends to a stable value in 10 cycles. From the 60th cycle to the 110th cycle, there is a clear upward trend in capacity. Then, the reversible capacity tends to stabilize, and a value of 612 mA h g−1 can be retained after 120 cycles. Capacity increase behaviors were also reported by other researchers regarding metal oxides and oxysalts. These interesting results could be due to the fact that some of the components act synergistically in ways we do not understand, as well as the existence of a possible activation process during the cycling of LIBs [46–49].

The rate capacities of the pristine FeCO3 and FeCO3/N-CD-15 anodes at different current densities (200, 400, 800, 1600, 3200, and 200 mA g−1) are presented in Fig. 5 . One can see that the FeCO3/N-CD composites deliver a better rate performance than the pristine FeCO3, especially at high current densities. When discharged/charged at a high current density of 3200 mA g−1, the FeCO3/N-CD-15 anode shows a capacity of 248 mA h g−1, and the value of the pristine FeCO3 is only 91 mA h g−1. When the current density returns from 3200 to 200 mA g−1, the capacity of the FeCO3/N-CD-15 anode increases from 248 to 580 mA h g−1, and the capacity shows an upward trend in the following cycles. From the 100th cycle to the 160th cycle, the capacity increases from 580 to 910 mA h g−1. The behavior of the capacity increase can be caused by the dramatic synergic effect of FeCO3 and N-CDs, along with a possible activation procedure [24]. For comparison, when the current density returns from 3200 to 200 mA g−1, the capacity of the FeCO3 anode is only 443 mA h g−1, and the capacity decreases to 339 mA h g−1 after 160 cycles. These results indicate that the rate performance and cycle stability of the FeCO3/N-CD-15 anode are significantly enhanced. It is likely that N-CDs attached to the surface of the FeCO3 create new Li+ transport paths, facilitate the rapid transfer of Li+ and electrons, and impair the volume expansions of the anode material in the lithiation/delithiation process.

Fig. 6 a presents the CV curves of the primary four cycles at a scanning rate of 200 mV s−1. It is obvious that the first CV curve is different from the others. In the first cathodic scan, there are two reduction peaks of 1.27 and 0.53 V. The peak at 1.27 V can be attributed to the slight intercalation of Li+ without structural changes [22,50]. The strong and broad peak at approximately 0.53 V is primarily the combination of the following four chemical reactions [51–53]: (1) the lithiation and decomposition of FeCO3 to Fe0 and Li2CO3 (Eqn (1)); (2) the reaction of Li2CO3 further reacts with Li to generate Li2O and LixC2 (Eqn (2)) under the electrocatalysis of newly produced Fe0 nanoparticles (researchers have reported that the lithium storage reaction of Li2CO3 takes place in the presence of newly formed metal nanoparticles (such as Sn and Si) as catalysts [54,55]); (3) the formation of an SEI film at the surface of the FeCO3/N-CDs during the initial discharging process; (4) the generation of Li2O and Fe0 from the reaction between the trace Fe2O3 impurities and Li+. In the anodic scan, the broad peaks at 1.09 and 1.60 V are due to the oxidation of metallic Fe0 to Fe2+ and the deposition of Li2CO3 [22,56]. In the second cycle, the cathodic peak shifts to 0.80 V, and the broad anodic peaks shift to 1.20 and 1.65 V, corresponding to the reversible change in the iron oxidation state. Note that the cathodic peak at 0.6 V in the second scan is also assigned to the irreversible reaction of SEI film formation, which disappears in the subsequent cycles. From the second cycle to the fourth cycle, the decreased intensity of the peaks may be caused by the partial damage of the crystal structure of FeCO3 nanorods during lithiation/delithiation, which coincides with the reported results for TMOs and ferrous carbonates [22,40].

As shown in Fig. 6b, the 20th, 60th, 80th, 120th, 180th and 300th cycles have similarly shaped discharge/charge curves, demonstrating that no new reversible redox reactions occur. Fig. 6c and d present the differential capacity plots in charge region Ⅱ and discharge region I for the 20th, 60th, 80th, 120th, 180th and 300th cycles, respectively. Note that the cathodic peak ca. 0.90 V and the anodic peak ca. 1.6 V are attributed to the reduction processes of Fe2+ to Fe0 and the oxidation processes of Fe0 to Fe2+ [56,57]. Interestingly, FeCO3/N-CD-15 presents a gradually increasing capacity after 60 cycles, as mentioned above. In Fig. 6e, the capacity of the FeCO3/N-CD-15 anode reaches 872 mA h g−1 for the whole process, which is more than twice as high as that in the 10th cycle. This attractive phenomenon is ascribed to the significant synergic effect of some of the components in the anode, as well as the existence of a possible activation process during the cycling of LIBs, which is consistent with many results reported for metal carbonate materials [46–49]. The Li+ transport process in the FeCO3/N-CD composite is shown in Fig. 6f. The addition of N-CDs increases the number of active sites for the transfer of Li+ and electrons. The high rate capability and excellent cycling stability can be ascribed to the hierarchical porous structure of the FeCO3/N-CD-15 composite. Such a unique structure of the FeCO3/N-CD-15 may facilitate the transfer of Li+ and electrons and impair volume expansions of the anode material during the lithiation/delithiation process.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed for both FeCO3/N-CDs and pristine FeCO3 anodes after 5 cycles (Fig. 7 a). The impedance spectra can be explained on the basis of the equivalent circuit shown in the inset of Fig. 7a. All the Nyquist plots are composed of a semicircle in the high-frequency range and an inclined line in the low-frequency region, which are attributed to the charge-transfer process and the diffusion of Li+, respectively [23]. The former corresponds to the charge transfer resistance (Rct). The Rct values of FeCO3, FeCO3/N-CD-5, FeCO3/N-CD-10, FeCO3/N-CD-15 and FeCO3/N-CD-20 are determined to be 143, 132, 130, 102, and 95 Ω, respectively. Obviously, the FeCO3/N-CD composites exhibit lower resistances. Moreover, the EIS measurements of the FeCO3/N-CD-15 anode for various cycles at 200 mA g−1 were performed, as depicted in Fig. 7b. The Rct of FeCO3/N-CD-15 of the first cycle is 93 Ω, and then it increases to 132 Ω due to the formation of SEI films (the 5th cycle). After 80 cycles, the Rct value decreases, and after 180 cycles, it recovers to 92 Ω. The results agree with the long-term trends of capacity, which are possible due to the synergistic effects of components and a possible activation process, as proposed in the previous reports [46–49,58]. It can be inferred that the incorporation of N-CDs to FeCO3 is effective in reducing the charge-transfer resistance.

The nitrogen-doped carbon dots were synthesized and used to prepare FeCO3/N-CD composites with the N-CD contents of 10 wt %, 15 wt % and 20 wt %. When the N-CD content was 15 wt %, a uniform dispersion of N-CD nanoparticles was achieved in the composite FeCO3/N-CD-15. Compared with the pristine FeCO3, a much smaller size of FeCO3 nanorods with a larger specific surface area were obtained in FeCO3/N-CD-15 composite. It is found that during the growth process of the FeCO3 crystal, N-CDs can inhibit the crystal growth and protect nanorods from tight stacking, finally forming the hierarchical porous nanocomposites. FeCO3/N-CD-15 delivered significantly faster transfer of Li+ and electrons, and lower volume expansions of the anode material in the lithiation/delithiation process. As a result, FeCO3/N-CD-15 exhibited a high reversible capacity of 872 mA h g−1 after 460 cycles at a current density of 200 mA g−1. Our study shows that the incorporation of N-CDs to FeCO3 is effective in improving LIB performances in all respects. The FeCO3/N-CD-15 is promising for high-performance LIBs as an anode material.

Bin Jiang: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Investigation, Validation. Yeru Liang: Methodology. Xiaoyuan Yu: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Gang Yuan: Conceptualization, Validation. Mingtao Zheng: Methodology, Formal analysis. Yong Xiao: Project administration. Hanwu Dong: Project administration. Yingliang Liu: Resources, Supervision. Hang Hu: Methodology, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financial supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2017A030313083), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21571066) and the Key Laboratory of Low-carbon Chemistry & Energy Conservation of Guangdong Province.
